---
title: "Breaking the Syntax Barrier"
subtitle: "Empowering Students to Code in Any Language"
author: "Marie Neubrander & Kat Husar"
date: "ECOTS 2026"
format: revealjs
---

## Welcome {.center}

:
**Breaking the Syntax Barrier**

*Empowering Students to Code in Any Language*

::: {.small}
ECOTS 2026 · Breakout Session
:::

::: {.notes}
- Smile, wave, name + institution.
- "Today we want to talk about something we've all watched happen: a student who's perfectly capable in one language sees a job posting in another, and self-rejects."
- Mention this will be interactive — encourage chat use throughout.
- Have co-host ready to monitor chat.
:::

---

## The scene {.center}


> *A student fluent in R sees "Python required" in a job posting…*
>
> *…and closes the tab.*

::: {.fragment}
They didn't lack the **statistics**.
They lacked confidence in the **syntax**.
:::

::: {.notes}
- Tell the real story (Marie or collaborator picks one — pick one you've actually seen).
- Pause after "closes the tab" — let it land.
- Pivot: this isn't a knowledge gap. It's a confidence gap.
:::

---

## Same job. Different syntax. {background-color="#fff7ed"}

Three real **entry-level Data Analyst** postings. Same kind of role —
look at what each one "requires."

::: {.job-row}
::: {}
![](images/Disney.png)

**Disney** — SQL & Python
:::

::: {}
![](images/Haystack.png)

**Haystack** — Excel, SQL, Tableau, Python, *or* R
:::

::: {}
![](images/Netflix.png)

**Netflix** — Python/R + SQL
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Same job. Three different "required" stacks.**
A student who only knows R isn't underqualified — they're one prompt away.
:::

::: {.notes}
- These are real postings, pulled while prepping this talk — not hypothetical.
- Point at the highlighted line in each: same "data analyst" role, different
  language combinations listed as requirements.
- Ties directly back to "The scene": this is the exact moment a student self-rejects.
- Land the punchline: the underlying skill (query, summarize, model data) is
  identical across all three — only the syntax differs.
:::

---

## Quick poll {background-color="#f0f9ff"}

### Which best describes your students?

a. **R-first** (intro stats, biostats, social science)
b. **Python-first** (CS-adjacent, data science programs)
c. **Mixed** — they see both
d. **Neither yet** — pre-programming

::: {.notes}
- Pre-build this poll in Zoom web portal BEFORE the session.
- ~30 sec to launch + vote, ~20 sec to read results.
- Tie results to the demo: "Most of you said R-first — so we'll start there."
:::

---

## "Fear of the new" {background-color="#fff7ed"}


The barrier isn't conceptual. It's **psychological**.

::: {.two-col}
::: {}
**Students who know:**

- What a linear model *is*
- How to interpret coefficients
- When to use a t-test
:::

::: {}
**Freeze when they see:**

- `model.fit(X, y)` instead of `lm(y ~ x)`
- `pd.DataFrame` instead of `data.frame`
- `:=` instead of `<-`
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Same concept. Different costume.**
:::

::: {.notes}
- This framing — "same concept, different costume" — is the through-line. Repeat it in the demo.
- Mention groups disproportionately affected: career-switchers, first-gen students, students from teaching-focused programs without industry exposure.
:::

---

## Chat waterfall {background-color="#f0f9ff"}

### Type — but **don't hit send yet**

> *What's one language or tool your students fear?*

::: {.fragment}
**3 … 2 … 1 … send.**
:::

::: {.notes}
- The "don't send yet" trick produces a visible wave instead of a trickle.
- Read 3-4 aloud. Look for patterns ("lots of SQL — we'll touch that").
- Co-host can screenshot the chat for the GitHub repo later.
:::

---

## The thesis {.center background-color="#ecfdf5"}

### AI can lower the syntax barrier

…**if** we design the activity so students learn the *mapping*,
not just receive the *answer*.

[Bridge]{.bridge} → toward mastery
[Crutch]{.crutch} → away from it

::: {.notes}
- Plant the bridge/crutch language. It comes back in section 5.
:::

---

## Demo: prompt-building {background-color="#fafafa"}

::: {.timing}
6:30 – 12:30 · DEMO (6 min)
:::

### Two prompts. Same task. Very different output.

We'll translate:

1. `lm()` in R → `LinearRegression` in Python
2. `pandas` groupby → `dplyr` pipe
3. A 30-second SQL ↔ tidyverse cameo

::: {.notes}
- Open ChatGPT/Claude in a separate window BEFORE you start so screenshare is ready.
- Have a pre-recorded backup video of this demo in case the AI is slow.
- Talk through what you're doing — don't just type silently.
:::

---

## Bad prompt {background-color="#fef2f2"}

::: {.timing}
6:30 – 8:00 · DEMO part 1 (1.5 min)
:::

```
Convert this R code to Python:

lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)
```

::: {.fragment}
**You get:** working code.

**You don't get:** why `~` became a design matrix, why
`sklearn` separates `X` and `y`, or that `summary()`
has no direct equivalent.
:::

::: {.notes}
- Run this live. Show the output.
- Pause on what's MISSING: the conceptual map.
- "This produces an answer. It doesn't produce learning."
:::

---

## Good prompt {background-color="#f0fdf4"}

::: {.timing}
8:00 – 10:00 · DEMO part 2 (2 min)
:::

```
I'm an intro stats student fluent in R.
Translate this R code to Python, AND:

1. Add inline comments mapping each R line to its Python equivalent.
2. Flag any idiom differences (e.g., formula interface vs. design matrix).
3. End with one concept-check question I should be able to answer
   before I move on.

R code:
lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)
```

::: {.notes}
- Run this live. Show the output — it should include comments + a check question.
- This is the centerpiece pattern. Name it: "learning goal + mapping + idiom flags + concept check."
- This pattern is in the GitHub repo as a template.
:::

---

## Python → R: same trick {background-color="#f0fdf4"}

::: {.timing}
10:00 – 11:30 · DEMO part 3 (1.5 min)
:::

```python
df.groupby("cyl")["mpg"].mean()
```

::: {.fragment}
**AI maps it to:**

```r
df |> group_by(cyl) |> summarize(mean_mpg = mean(mpg))
```

…and explains that method chaining ↔ the pipe.
:::

::: {.notes}
- This is the tidyverse cameo. Highlight that the pipe is the same idea as `.` chaining in pandas.
- Students who learned dplyr first often "get" pandas faster once they see this.
:::

---

## 30-sec SQL cameo {background-color="#f0fdf4"}

::: {.timing}
11:30 – 12:30 · DEMO part 4 (1 min)
:::

::: {.two-col}
::: {}
**SQL**
```sql
SELECT cyl, AVG(mpg)
FROM mtcars
GROUP BY cyl
HAVING AVG(mpg) > 20;
```
:::

::: {}
**dplyr**
```r
mtcars |>
  group_by(cyl) |>
  summarize(m = mean(mpg)) |>
  filter(m > 20)
```
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**One concept. Three costumes.**
:::

::: {.notes}
- Move fast. Don't explain SQL in depth — just show that GROUP BY/HAVING maps cleanly.
- This is the moment the "same concept, different costume" line earns its keep.
:::

---

## Activity {background-color="#f0f9ff"}

::: {.timing}
12:30 – 17:30 · HANDS-ON (5 min)
:::

### Your turn

You'll take a code snippet, write a prompt that
produces a **pedagogically useful** translation,
and evaluate it.

::: {.fragment}
**Rubric:**

1. Does it run?
2. Does it *teach the mapping*?
3. Does it flag idiom differences?
4. Does it leave the student able to do the next problem alone?
:::

::: {.notes}
- Format is flexible — see backup slide for options.
- DEFAULT format: shared Padlet/Google Doc. Drop link in chat.
- If running Padlet: have 4 snippets pre-loaded as columns. Attendees pick one.
- Set a 4-min timer. Call attention back at 4 min.
:::

---

## Activity — what to do {background-color="#f0f9ff"}

::: {.timing}
(visible during the 5-min activity)
:::

::: {.small}
**Link:** [padlet link / Google Doc link goes here]
:::

1. Pick a snippet (R, Python, or SQL).
2. Write a prompt using the template:
   *learning goal · mapping · idiom flags · concept check*
3. Paste the AI output.
4. Score 1–4 on the rubric. Drop one insight in the chat.

::: {.notes}
- Leave this slide up while attendees work.
- Co-host watches chat for early finishers; you watch the Padlet.
- At the 4-min mark: "30 seconds — wrap up your score."
:::

---

## What patterns showed up? {background-color="#f0f9ff"}

::: {.timing}
17:30 – 18:30 · DEBRIEF (1 min)
:::

- Which prompts worked best?
- Where did AI over-explain or under-explain?
- Which snippets surprised people?

::: {.notes}
- Narrate live from what you saw in the Padlet/chat.
- Pick 2-3 to call out by name (if comfortable) or anonymously.
- Bridge to next section: "This is how we tell bridge from crutch."
:::

---

## Bridge vs. crutch {background-color="#ecfdf5"}

::: {.timing}
18:30 – 22:30 · GUARDRAILS (4 min)
:::

::: {.two-col}
::: {}
[**Bridge**]{.bridge}

- Annotate AI output
- Translate, then close the tab and re-derive
- Pair with a concept check
- Scaffolded fading (week 1 → week 5)
:::

::: {}
[**Crutch**]{.crutch}

- Copy-paste without reading
- Skip the *why*
- Never reproduce unaided
- Same level of help every week
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
- This is the most "Monday-morning useful" section. Slow down.
- Each bullet is a real classroom move — give one quick example for each on the left.
- "Scaffolded fading" = the key term they'll Google later.
:::

---

## Who benefits most? {background-color="#ecfdf5"}

::: {.timing}
22:30 – 23:30 · WHO (1 min)
:::

**Benefits most:**

- Students switching ecosystems mid-program
- Career-switchers with stats but new to programming
- Students at teaching-focused institutions w/o industry exposure

**At risk of over-reliance:**

- Students who haven't built the underlying concept yet
- Students using AI to skip — not to bridge

::: {.notes}
- Be honest: AI translation is most useful AFTER the concept is solid in one language.
- Don't introduce it as a first encounter with linear regression.
:::

---

## Quick poll {background-color="#f0f9ff"}

::: {.timing}
23:30 – 24:00 · POLL #2 (30 sec)
:::

### Which guardrail will you try first?

a. Annotate AI output
b. Translate-then-close-the-tab
c. AI + concept-check pairing
d. Scaffolded fading across the term

::: {.notes}
- Quick poll, no deep discussion. Just commitment.
- Read top result aloud.
:::

---

## Take this with you {background-color="#fefce8"}

::: {.timing}
24:00 – 25:00 · CLOSE (1 min)
:::

### Three things for Monday

1. **One prompt template** — *goal · mapping · idiom · check*
2. **One guardrail** — pick the one you just voted for
3. **One concept** you'll teach with translation next week

::: {.small}
**Repo:** github.com/mneubrander/ecots-2026 ·
prompt templates + R↔Python examples + rubric
:::

::: {.notes}
- Drop repo link in chat NOW (have it pre-copied).
- "If you remember one thing: same concept, different costume."
- Thank co-host, attendees. Open for Q&A.
:::

---

## Q&A {.center}

### Questions?

::: {.small}
**Marie Neubrander** — [email]
**Kat Husar** — [email]
**Repo:** github.com/mneubrander/ecots-2026
:::

::: {.notes}
- Most likely Q's:
  - "What AI tool do you use?" → answer honestly, mention free tiers
  - "How do you handle students who just copy-paste?" → scaffolded fading + concept checks
  - "Does this work for [language X]?" → yes, same prompt pattern
  - "What about academic integrity?" → frame as a *learning tool*, like a calculator; disclosure norms
:::

---

# Backup slides {.center background-color="#1f2937" style="color: white;"}

::: {.notes}
- Anything past here is overflow / Q&A support / safety net.
:::

---

## Activity format options {background-color="#fafafa"}

| Format | Setup | Risk | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Padlet / Google Doc** | 5 min | Low | Default; any room size |
| **Zoom breakouts** | 2 min mid-session | Medium | Deeper engagement, ≥15 attendees |
| **Chat-driven live demo** | None | Higher | Small/known room |
| **Custom API website** | 5+ days | **Too high** | Don't ship in 5 days |

::: {.notes}
- This slide is for YOU during planning, not for the audience.
- Decide format 24h before based on registration count.
:::

---

## If demo breaks {background-color="#fef2f2"}

1. Switch to the **pre-recorded demo video** (have it open in a tab).
2. Or pivot to: *"Let's read this output together"* with a printed/slide screenshot of a known-good run.
3. Don't troubleshoot live for more than 30 seconds.

::: {.notes}
- Pre-record a 90-sec backup video of the good-prompt demo.
- Save 3-4 known-good screenshots as slides 28-31 (TODO).
:::

---

## Prompt template (full) {background-color="#f0fdf4"}

```
I'm a [level] student fluent in [source language].
Translate this [source] code to [target], AND:

1. Add inline comments mapping each [source] line to [target].
2. Flag any idiom differences (formulas, indexing,
   data structures, default behaviors).
3. End with one concept-check question I should be
   able to answer before moving on.
4. Do NOT explain basic syntax I already know in [source].

Code:
[paste here]
```

::: {.notes}
- This is the full version of the template — the slides earlier showed a stats-specific instantiation.
- The "do NOT explain basic syntax" line is what stops AI from being condescending.
:::
